chapter.title: Nuclei
chapter.summary:
This chapter on Nuclei explores the structure, properties, and behavior of atomic nuclei. It begins by establishing the composition of the nucleus – protons and neutrons – and defining key terms like atomic number, mass number, isotopes, isobars, and isotones. Crucially, it introduces the concept of the atomic mass unit (u) for quantifying the mass of atomic particles. The chapter then delves into the size of the nucleus, presenting an empirical formula for calculating nuclear radius based on mass number.

A central concept is mass-energy equivalence (E=mc²) and its application to nuclear binding energy. The mass defect, the difference between the mass of a nucleus and the sum of its constituent nucleons, reveals the energy holding the nucleus together. The binding energy per nucleon is analyzed, highlighting the stability of mid-range nuclei and explaining the principles behind nuclear fission and fusion. The chapter details the nuclear force, a strong, short-range, and charge-independent force.

Radioactivity, the spontaneous decay of unstable nuclei, is discussed, differentiating between alpha, beta, and gamma decay. The chapter culminates in an exploration of nuclear energy, explaining the energy release in fission and fusion reactions. The proton-proton cycle in stars is presented as an example of fusion. The chapter ends with the practical applications of nuclear physics in energy production and includes quantitative examples to illustrate key concepts and formulas. The final section provides a useful overview of fundamental constants and problem-solving strategies in nuclear physics.

subsections.title: The Discovery and Nature of the Nucleus
subsections.content_text: In every atom, the positive charge and mass are densely concentrated at the centre, forming the nucleus. The dimensions of a nucleus are significantly smaller than those of the atom itself. Experiments involving the scattering of $\alpha$-particles have demonstrated that the radius of a nucleus is smaller than the radius of an atom by a factor of approximately $10^4$. Consequently, the volume of a nucleus is about $10^{-12}$ times the volume of the atom, implying that an atom is mostly empty space. To visualize this, if an atom were enlarged to the size of a classroom, the nucleus would be roughly the size of a pinhead. Despite its small size, the nucleus contains more than $99.9\%$ of the total mass of the atom. This chapter explores the structure of the nucleus, its constituents, and phenomena such as radioactivity, fission, and fusion.

sections.title: Atomic Masses and Composition of Nucleus

subsections.title: The Atomic Mass Unit (u)
subsections.content_text: Because the mass of an atom is extremely small (e.g., a carbon atom $^{12}C$ is $1.992647 \times 10^{-26}$ kg), the kilogram is an inconvenient unit. Instead, the atomic mass unit (u) is used. It is defined as $1/12^{th}$ of the mass of the carbon ($^{12}C$) atom. According to this definition: $$1u = \frac{\text{mass of one }^{12}C \text{ atom}}{12} = \frac{1.992647 \times 10^{-26} \text{ kg}}{12} = 1.660539 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$$ Atomic masses of elements are often close to integral multiples of the hydrogen atom's mass, though there are notable exceptions like chlorine ($35.46$ u).

subsections.title: Isotopes and Average Atomic Mass
subsections.content_text: Measurement of atomic masses using mass spectrometers reveals that atoms of the same element can have different masses while maintaining the same chemical properties. These species are called isotopes. Most elements exist as a mixture of isotopes. For example, chlorine consists of two isotopes with masses $34.98$ u and $36.98$ u, with relative abundances of $75.4\%$ and $24.6\%$, respectively. The average atomic mass is calculated as a weighted average: $$\text{Average mass} = \frac{75.4 \times 34.98 + 24.6 \times 36.98}{100} = 35.47 \text{ u}$$ Hydrogen also has isotopes: protium ($1.0078$ u), deuterium ($2.0141$ u), and tritium ($3.0160$ u). Tritium is unstable and does not occur naturally.

subsections.title: The Proton and Nuclear Charge
subsections.content_text: The nucleus of the lightest hydrogen atom is called the proton. Its mass is $m_p = 1.00727 \text{ u} = 1.67262 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$. This mass is approximately the mass of a hydrogen atom minus the mass of an electron ($m_e = 0.00055$ u). Protons carry a single unit of fundamental positive charge ($+e$) and are stable. Since an atom is electrically neutral and contains $Z$ electrons (where $Z$ is the atomic number) with total charge $-Ze$, the nucleus must have a total charge of $+Ze$. This confirms that the number of protons in the nucleus is exactly $Z$.

subsections.title: Discovery of the Neutron
subsections.content_text: The existence of isotopes like deuterium (mass ratio 2:1 compared to hydrogen) suggested the presence of neutral matter in the nucleus. In 1932, James Chadwick discovered the neutron by bombarding beryllium nuclei with $\alpha$-particles. He observed the emission of neutral radiation that could knock out protons from light nuclei. By applying the principles of conservation of energy and momentum, Chadwick determined that this radiation consisted of neutral particles, which he named neutrons, with a mass nearly equal to that of a proton. Chadwick was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1935 for this discovery.

subsections.title: Properties of the Neutron
subsections.content_text: The mass of a neutron is $m_n = 1.00866 \text{ u} = 1.6749 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$. Unlike a free proton, a free neutron is unstable. It decays into a proton, an electron, and an antineutrino, with a mean life of approximately $1000$ s. However, neutrons are stable when they are contained within a nucleus.

subsections.title: Nuclear Composition and Notation
subsections.content_text:
The composition of a nucleus is defined by three primary symbols: 
1. $Z$ (Atomic Number): The number of protons.
2. $N$ (Neutron Number): The number of neutrons.
3. $A$ (Mass Number): The total number of protons and neutrons ($A = Z + N$).

Protons and neutrons are collectively referred to as nucleons. A specific nuclear species or nuclide is represented by the notation $^A_Z X$, where $X$ is the chemical symbol. For example, a gold nucleus is denoted as $^{197}_{79}Au$, indicating it contains $197$ nucleons, of which $79$ are protons and $118$ are neutrons.

subsections.title: Isotopes, Isobars, and Isotones
subsections.content_text: The composition of isotopes of an element can be explained by the number of neutrons in the nucleus. Nuclei of isotopes of a given element contain the same number of protons ($Z$) but differ in their number of neutrons ($N$). For example, Hydrogen has isotopes: Protium ($^1_1\text{H}$), Deuterium ($^2_1\text{H}$ with 1 proton and 1 neutron), and Tritium ($^3_1\text{H}$ with 1 proton and 2 neutrons). Gold ($Z=79$) has 32 isotopes ranging from $A=173$ to $A=204$. Since chemical properties depend on the electronic structure (determined by $Z$), isotopes exhibit identical chemical behavior and occupy the same position in the periodic table. Nuclides with the same mass number $A$ are called isobars. For instance, $^3_1\text{H}$ and $^3_2\text{He}$ are isobars. Nuclides with the same neutron number $N$ but different atomic number $Z$ are called isotones. Examples include $^{198}_{80}\text{Hg}$ and $^{197}_{79}\text{Au}$, both of which have $N = A - Z = 118$.

subsections.title: Rutherford's Scattering and Nuclear Size
subsections.content_text: Rutherford's alpha-particle scattering experiments established the existence of the atomic nucleus. In the Geiger-Marsden experiment, the distance of closest approach of a 5.5 MeV $\alpha$-particle to a gold nucleus was found to be approximately $4.0 \times 10^{-14}$ m. Since the scattering was attributed to Coulomb repulsion between positive charges, the actual size of the nucleus must be smaller than this distance. At higher energies, $\alpha$-particles can penetrate closer to the nucleus, where the scattering is affected by short-range nuclear forces. Deviations from Rutherford's calculations (based purely on Coulomb repulsion) allow for the inference of nuclear sizes.

subsections.title: Empirical Formula for Nuclear Radius and Density
subsections.content_text: Accurate measurements using fast electrons as projectiles have shown that the radius $R$ of a nucleus with mass number $A$ is given by the empirical relation: $R = R_0 A^{1/3}$ (13.5), where $R_0 = 1.2 \times 10^{-15}$ m = 1.2 fm. This relationship implies that the volume of the nucleus ($V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3$) is directly proportional to the mass number $A$. Consequently, the density of nuclear matter is constant and independent of $A$ for all nuclei. The density is approximately $2.3 \times 10^{17}$ kg m$^{-3}$. This value is extremely high compared to ordinary matter (e.g., water at $10^3$ kg m$^{-3}$), reflecting the fact that most of the atom's volume is empty space, with mass concentrated in the tiny nucleus. Different nuclei can be envisioned as drops of liquid with constant density.

subsections.title: Example 13.1: Nuclear Density of Iron
subsections.content_text: This example calculates the density of an iron nucleus to demonstrate the extreme concentration of mass in nuclei and its comparison to astrophysical objects like neutron stars.

worked_examples.label: Example 13.1
worked_examples.problem: Given the mass of iron nucleus as 55.85u and A=56, find the nuclear density?
worked_examples.solution: $m_{Fe} = 55.85 u$, $u = 1.66 \times 10^{-27}$ kg. Mass of nucleus $m = 55.85 \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27} = 9.27 \times 10^{-26}$ kg. Volume $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi (R_0 A^{1/3})^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi R_0^3 A = \frac{4}{3} \pi (1.2 \times 10^{-15})^3 \times 56$. Nuclear density = $\frac{\text{mass}}{\text{volume}} = \frac{9.27 \times 10^{-26}}{(4\pi/3)(1.2 \times 10^{-15})^3 \times 56} = 2.29 \times 10^{17}$ kg m$^{-3}$. The density of matter in neutron stars is comparable to this density, showing that matter in these objects has been compressed to such an extent that they resemble a big nucleus.

subsections.title: Mass-Energy Equivalence
subsections.content_text: Einstein's theory of special relativity established that mass is another form of energy. Previously, it was presumed that mass and energy were conserved separately. Einstein showed that mass is another form of energy and one can convert mass-energy into other forms of energy, say kinetic energy and vice-versa. Einstein gave the famous mass-energy equivalence relation: $E = mc^2$ (13.6), where $E$ is the energy equivalent of mass $m$, and $c$ is the velocity of light in vacuum (approximately $3 \times 10^8$ m s$^{-1}$). Experimental verification has been achieved in nuclear reactions amongst nucleons, nuclei, and other particles. The conservation law of energy states that the initial energy and the final energy are equal provided the energy associated with mass is also included.

subsections.title: Example 13.2: Energy Equivalent of 1g
subsections.content_text: This example illustrates the enormous amount of energy released if a small amount of matter is converted into energy.

worked_examples.label: Example 13.2
worked_examples.problem: Calculate the energy equivalent of 1 g of substance.
worked_examples.solution: Energy, $E = 10^{-3} \times (3 \times 10^8)^2$ J = $10^{-3} \times 9 \times 10^{16} = 9 \times 10^{13}$ J. Thus, if one gram of matter is converted to energy, there is a release of enormous amount of energy.

subsections.title: Nuclear Binding Energy and Mass Defect
subsections.content_text:
A nucleus is composed of protons and neutrons. It is expected that the mass of a nucleus equals the sum of the masses of its individual constituents. However, the nuclear mass $M$ is found to be always less than this. For example, in $^{16}_8\text{O}$ (8 protons, 8 neutrons), the expected mass is $8 \times (1.00866 + 1.00727) = 16.12744$ u. The experimental mass of the nucleus is 15.99053 u. The difference in mass, $\Delta M$, is called the mass defect: $\Delta M = [Z m_p + (A - Z) m_n] - M$ (13.7). This mass difference corresponds to an energy $E_b = \Delta M c^2$ (13.8), called the binding energy. If one wants to break the oxygen nucleus into 8 protons and 8 neutrons, this extra energy $\Delta M c^2$ has to be supplied. If a certain number of neutrons and protons are brought together to form a nucleus of a certain charge and mass, an energy $E_b$ will be released
This example calculates the energy equivalent of one atomic mass unit and applies it to find the binding energy of an oxygen nucleus.

worked_examples.label: Example 13.3
worked_examples.problem: Find the energy equivalent of one atomic mass unit, first in Joules and then in MeV. Using this, express the mass defect of $^{16}_8\text{O}$ in MeV/c$^2$.
worked_examples.solution: $1 u = 1.6605 \times 10^{-27}$ kg. Energy equivalent = $1.6605 \times 10^{-27} \times (2.9979 \times 10^8)^2$ kg m$^2$/s$^2$ = $1.4924 \times 10^{-10}$ J. In eV: $\frac{1.4924 \times 10^{-10}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$ eV = $0.9315 \times 10^9$ eV = 931.5 MeV. Or, $1 u = 931.5$ MeV/c$^2$. For $^{16}_8\text{O}$, $\Delta M = 0.13691$ u = $0.13691 \times 931.5$ MeV/c$^2$ = 127.5 MeV/c$^2$. The energy needed to separate $^{16}_8\text{O}$ into its constituents is thus 127.5 MeV/c$^2$.

subsections.title: Binding Energy per Nucleon
subsections.content_text: A more useful measure of the binding between the constituents of the nucleus is the binding energy per nucleon, $E_{bn}$, which is the ratio of the binding energy $E_b$ of a nucleus to the number of the nucleons, $A$, in that nucleus: $$E_{bn} = E_b / A$$ (13.9). We can think of binding energy per nucleon as the average energy per nucleon needed to separate a nucleus into its individual nucleons.

subsections.title: Analysis of the Binding Energy Curve
subsections.content_text: Figure 13.1 is a plot of the binding energy per nucleon $E_{bn}$ versus the mass number $A$ for a large number of nuclei. The main features of the plot are: (i) The binding energy per nucleon, $E_{bn}$, is practically constant, i.e., practically independent of the atomic number for nuclei of middle mass number ($30 < A < 170$). The curve has a maximum of about $8.75$ MeV for $A = 56$ and has a value of $7.6$ MeV for $A = 238$. (ii) $E_{bn}$ is lower for both light nuclei ($A < 30$) and heavy nuclei ($A > 170$).

diagrams.label: FIGURE 13.1
diagrams.description: A graph showing the binding energy per nucleon (in MeV) as a function of mass number (A). The curve rises sharply for light nuclei, shows peaks for specific nuclei like 4He, 12C, and 16O, reaches a broad maximum around A=56 (Fe), and then gradually decreases for heavier nuclei like 238U.

subsections.title: Conclusions: Nuclear Force and Saturation
subsections.content_text: From the binding energy curve, we can conclude: (i) The nuclear force is attractive and sufficiently strong to produce a binding energy of a few MeV per nucleon. (ii) The constancy of the binding energy in the range $30 < A < 170$ is a consequence of the fact that the nuclear force is short-ranged. In a large nucleus, a nucleon is under the influence of only its immediate neighbors within the range of the nuclear force. If a nucleon has a maximum of $p$ neighbors, its binding energy would be proportional to $p$. Let the binding energy be $pk$, where $k$ is a constant. Increasing $A$ by adding nucleons does not change the binding energy of an internal nucleon because most nucleons reside inside and not on the surface. This property, where a nucleon influences only those close to it, is referred to as the saturation property of the nuclear force.

subsections.title: Implications for Nuclear Fission and Fusion
subsections.content_text: (iii) A very heavy nucleus, say $A = 240$, has lower binding energy per nucleon compared to a nucleus with $A = 120$. Thus, if a nucleus $A = 240$ breaks into two $A = 120$ nuclei, the nucleons get more tightly bound, and energy is released in the process of fission. (iv) Consider two very light nuclei ($A \leq 10$) joining to form a heavier nucleus. The binding energy per nucleon of the fused heavier nuclei is more than that of the lighter nuclei, meaning the final system is more tightly bound. Energy is released in such a process of fusion, which is the energy source of the sun.

subsections.title: Introduction to the Nuclear Force
subsections.content_text: The force that determines the motion of atomic electrons is the Coulomb force. However, to bind a nucleus together, there must be a strong attractive force of a totally different kind to overcome the Coulomb repulsion between positively charged protons and bind both protons and neutrons into the tiny nuclear volume. This nuclear binding force is much stronger than the Coulomb force.

subsections.title: Properties of the Nuclear Force
subsections.content_text: Key features of the nuclear force include: (i) It is much stronger than the Coulomb force or gravitational force. It must dominate the Coulomb repulsive force between protons inside the nucleus. (ii) The nuclear force between two nucleons falls rapidly to zero as their distance is more than a few femtometres. This leads to the saturation of forces in a medium or large-sized nucleus, explaining the constancy of binding energy per nucleon.

subsections.title: Potential Energy and Separation
subsections.content_text: A plot of the potential energy between two nucleons as a function of distance is shown in Fig. 13.2. The potential energy is a minimum at a distance $r_0$ of about $0.8$ fm. This means that the force is attractive for distances larger than $0.8$ fm and strongly repulsive if they are separated by distances less than $0.8$ fm.

diagrams.label: FIGURE 13.2
diagrams.description: A graph of potential energy (MeV) of a pair of nucleons versus their separation r (fm). The curve shows a deep minimum at r0 ≈ 0.8 fm. For r > r0, the potential energy increases toward zero (attractive force), and for r < r0, it rises sharply (repulsive force).

subsections.title: Charge Independence of Nuclear Force
subsections.content_text: (iii) The nuclear force between neutron-neutron, proton-neutron, and proton-proton is approximately the same. The nuclear force does not depend on the electric charge. Unlike Coulomb's law or Newton's law of gravitation, there is no simple mathematical form for the nuclear force.

subsections.title: Discovery and Definition of Radioactivity
subsections.content_text: A. H. Becquerel discovered radioactivity in 1896 by accident while studying fluorescence and phosphorescence. He observed that uranium-potassium sulphate emitted radiation that could penetrate black paper and silver to blacken a photographic plate. Radioactivity is a nuclear phenomenon in which an unstable nucleus undergoes a decay, referred to as radioactive decay.

subsections.title: Types of Radioactive Decay
subsections.content_text: Three types of radioactive decay occur in nature: (i) $\alpha$-decay in which a helium nucleus $_2^4\text{He}$ is emitted; (ii) $\beta$-decay in which electrons or positrons (particles with the same mass as electrons but opposite charge) are emitted; (iii) $\gamma$-decay in which high energy (hundreds of keV or more) photons are emitted.

subsections.title: Energy Release in Nuclear Reactions
subsections.content_text: The binding energy curve shows that if nuclei with less total binding energy transform into nuclei with greater binding energy, there will be a net energy release. This occurs when a heavy nucleus decays into intermediate mass fragments (fission) or when light nuclei fuse into a heavier nucleus (fusion). While exothermic chemical reactions involve energies in the range of electron volts (eV), nuclear reactions involve energies of the order of MeV. For the same quantity of matter, nuclear sources produce a million times more energy than chemical sources. For example, fission of $1$ kg of uranium generates $10^{14}$ J of energy, compared to $10^7$ J from burning $1$ kg of coal. Nuclear fission is a process where a heavy nucleus, when bombarded by particles like neutrons, protons, or alpha particles, splits into two intermediate-mass fragments. A prominent example is the neutron-induced fission of Uranium-235 ($^{235}_{92}U$). This reaction can produce various pairs of fragments, such as Barium and Krypton, Antimony and Niobium, or Xenon and Strontium, along with additional neutrons. For instance: $^1_0n + ^{235}_{92}U \rightarrow ^{236}_{92}U \rightarrow ^{144}_{56}Ba + ^{89}_{36}Kr + 3^1_0n$. The resulting fragments are typically radioactive and undergo subsequent beta decays to reach stability. Controlled fission is the energy source for nuclear reactors, while uncontrolled fission powers atomic bombs.

subsections.title: Energy Release in Fission
subsections.content_text: The energy released in a fission reaction, known as the $Q$ value, is approximately $200$ MeV per nucleus. This can be estimated by analyzing the binding energy per nucleon ($E_{bn}$). For a nucleus with $A=240$ ($E_{bn} \approx 7.6$ MeV) splitting into two fragments with $A=120$ ($E_{bn} \approx 8.5$ MeV), there is a gain in binding energy of about $0.9$ MeV per nucleon. The total energy gain is $240 \times 0.9 = 216$ MeV. This energy initially manifests as the kinetic energy of the fragments and neutrons, eventually converting into heat as it is transferred to surrounding matter.

subsections.title: Nuclear Fusion
subsections.content_text:
Nuclear fusion involves the combination of two light nuclei to form a larger, more stable nucleus, releasing significant energy. This occurs because the product nucleus has a higher binding energy per nucleon than the reactants. Examples include the fusion of hydrogen isotopes to form deuterium: $^1_1H + ^1_1H \rightarrow ^2_1H + e^+ + \nu + 0.42$ MeV, and the fusion of deuterons to form helium or tritium: $^2_1H + ^2_1H \rightarrow ^3_2He + n + 3.27$ MeV or $^2_1H + ^2_1H \rightarrow ^3_1H + ^1_1H + 4.03$ MeV.
For fusion to occur, nuclei must come close enough for the short-range nuclear force to overcome the electrostatic Coulomb repulsion. This repulsion creates a barrier; for two protons, the barrier height is approximately $400$ keV. To overcome this, the nuclei must have high kinetic energy, which requires extreme temperatures. For a proton gas, the required temperature is estimated at $T \sim 3 \times 10^9$ K. Fusion achieved through such high temperatures is termed thermonuclear fusion.

subsections.title: The Proton-Proton Cycle
subsections.content_text: In stars like the Sun, energy is generated through the proton-proton ($p, p$) cycle, a multi-step process where hydrogen is converted into helium. The cycle involves: (i) $^1_1H + ^1_1H \rightarrow ^2_1H + e^+ + \nu + 0.42$ MeV, (ii) $e^+ + e^- \rightarrow \gamma + \gamma + 1.02$ MeV, (iii) $^2_1H + ^1_1H \rightarrow ^3_2He + \gamma + 5.49$ MeV, and (iv) $^3_2He + ^3_2He \rightarrow ^4_2He + ^1_1H + ^1_1H + 12.86$ MeV. The net effect is the fusion of four protons and two electrons into a helium nucleus, releasing $26.7$ MeV of energy: $4^1_1H + 2e^- \rightarrow ^4_2He + 2\nu + 6\gamma + 26.7$ MeV.

subsections.title: Stellar Evolution and the Sun
subsections.content_text: The Sun has an estimated age of $5 \times 10^9$ years and enough hydrogen to last another 5 billion years. Once hydrogen is depleted, the core will collapse under gravity, raising the temperature to $10^8$ K and initiating helium fusion into carbon. This process continues to form heavier elements until the peak of the binding energy curve is reached. Elements more massive than those near the peak cannot be produced this way. During this transition, the Sun will expand into a red giant.

subsections.title: Controlled Thermonuclear Fusion
subsections.content_text: Controlled fusion reactors aim to generate steady power by heating nuclear fuel to temperatures around $10^8$ K, creating a plasma of ions and electrons. The primary challenge is confining this high-temperature plasma, as no physical container can withstand it. International efforts, including those in India, are focused on developing techniques to achieve sustainable fusion, which would provide nearly unlimited energy.

subsections.title: Example 13.4: Balancing and Energy in Nuclear Reactions
subsections.content_text: This example clarifies the differences between chemical and nuclear reactions regarding conservation laws and mass-energy conversion. It explains that while chemical reactions balance atoms, nuclear reactions balance the number of protons and neutrons. It also addresses how mass defect relates to energy release even when nucleon count is constant. This subsection concludes the discussion on mass-energy interconversion by comparing nuclear and chemical reactions. In nuclear reactions, the difference in the total mass of nuclei on both sides is converted into energy. Similarly, in chemical reactions, the energy released or absorbed is linked to the difference in chemical (not nuclear) binding energies of atoms and molecules. Although chemical binding energy also contributes to a mass defect, the effect is nearly a million times smaller than in nuclear reactions, leading to the common misconception that mass-energy interconversion only occurs in nuclear processes.

worked_examples.label: Example 13.4
worked_examples.problem: Answer the following questions: (a) Are the equations of nuclear reactions (such as those given in Section 13.7) ‘balanced’ in the sense a chemical equation (e.g., $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2 H_2O$) is? If not, in what sense are they balanced on both sides? (b) If both the number of protons and the number of neutrons are conserved in each nuclear reaction, in what way is mass converted into energy (or vice-versa) in a nuclear reaction? (c) A general impression exists that mass-energy interconversion takes place only in nuclear reaction and never in chemical reaction. This is strictly speaking, incorrect. Explain.
worked_examples.solution: (a) A chemical equation is balanced in the sense that the number of atoms of each element is the same on both sides. A chemical reaction merely alters the original combinations of atoms. In a nuclear reaction, elements may be transmuted. Thus, the number of atoms of each element is not necessarily conserved. However, the number of protons and the number of neutrons are both separately conserved in a nuclear reaction. [Actually, even this is not strictly true in the realm of very high energies – what is strictly conserved is the total charge and total ‘baryon number’.] (b) We know that the binding energy of a nucleus gives a negative contribution to the mass of the nucleus (mass defect). Now, since proton number and neutron number are conserved in a nuclear reaction, the total rest mass of neutrons and protons is the same on either side of a reaction. But the total binding energy of nuclei on the left side need not be the same as that on the right hand side. The difference in these binding energies appears as energy released or absorbed in a nuclear reaction. Since binding energy [CONTINUES]

worked_examples.label: Example 13.4 (c)
worked_examples.problem: Explain why mass-energy interconversion is often associated only with nuclear reactions and not chemical reactions, despite being applicable to both.
worked_examples.solution: From the point of view of mass-energy interconversion, a chemical reaction is similar to a nuclear reaction in principle. The energy released or absorbed in a chemical reaction can be traced to the difference in chemical (not nuclear) binding energies of atoms and molecules on the two sides of a reaction. Since, strictly speaking, chemical binding energy also gives a negative contribution (mass defect) to the total mass of an atom or molecule, we can equally well say that the difference in the total mass of atoms or molecules, on the two sides of the chemical reaction gets converted into energy or vice-versa. However, the mass defects involved in a chemical reaction are almost a million times smaller than those in a nuclear reaction. This is the reason for the general impression, (which is incorrect) that mass-energy interconversion does not take place in a chemical reaction.

subsections.title: Atomic Nucleus and Mass Unit
subsections.content_text: An atom consists of a positively charged nucleus where more than $99.9\%$ of the atom's mass is concentrated. The radius of the nucleus is smaller than the atom's radius by a factor of $10^4$. On the atomic scale, mass is measured in atomic mass units ($u$), where $1 u$ is defined as $1/12^{th}$ the mass of a ${}^{12}C$ atom ($1 u = 1.660563 \times 10^{-27}$ kg). Nuclei also contain neutrons, which are neutral particles with a mass nearly equal to that of a proton.

subsections.title: Nuclide Classification and Isotopes
subsections.content_text: A nuclear species or nuclide is represented as ${}^A_Z X$, where $Z$ is the atomic number (number of protons), $A$ is the mass number (total protons and neutrons), and $N = A - Z$ is the number of neutrons. Nuclides with the same $Z$ but different $N$ are isotopes; those with the same $A$ are isobars; and those with the same $N$ are isotones. Most elements are mixtures of isotopes, and their atomic mass is a weighted average based on relative abundance.

subsections.title: Nuclear Size and Density
subsections.content_text: Nuclei are considered spherical with a radius $R$ that fits the formula $R = R_0 A^{1/3}$, where $R_0 \approx 1.2$ fm. This relationship implies that nuclear density is independent of the mass number $A$ and is approximately $10^{17}$ kg/m³.

subsections.title: Nuclear Force and Binding Energy
subsections.content_text: Neutrons and protons are bound by the short-range strong nuclear force, which is independent of charge. The nuclear mass $M$ is always less than the sum of the masses of its constituents $\sum m$. The difference is the mass defect $\Delta M = (Z m_p + (A - Z)m_n) - M$. The binding energy is $\Delta E_b = \Delta M c^2$. For $A$ between 30 and 170, the binding energy per nucleon is nearly constant at about $8$ MeV/nucleon.

subsections.title: Q-value and Energy Scales
subsections.content_text: The $Q$-value of a nuclear process is the difference between the final and initial kinetic energies, which by mass-energy conservation equals $Q = (\text{sum of initial masses} - \text{sum of final masses})c^2$. Nuclear processes involve energies about a million times larger than chemical processes.

subsections.title: Radioactivity, Fission, and Fusion
subsections.content_text: Radioactivity involves nuclei transforming by emitting $\alpha$ (helium nuclei), $\beta$ (electrons), or $\gamma$ (electromagnetic radiation) rays. Energy is released when less tightly bound nuclei transmute into more tightly bound ones. In fission, a heavy nucleus like ${}^{235}_{92}U$ breaks into smaller fragments (e.g., ${}^{235}_{92}U + {}^1_0n \to {}^{133}_{51}Sb + {}^{99}_{41}Nb + 4 {}^1_0n$). In fusion, light nuclei combine to form a larger nucleus, which is the energy source for stars.

subsections.title: Table of Physical Quantities
subsections.content_text: The following table summarizes key physical quantities in nuclear physics, including their symbols, dimensions, units, and relevant remarks.

tables.label: Table 13.1
tables.caption: Physical Quantities in Nuclear Physics
rows: Unit of mass for expressing atomic or nuclear masses. One atomic mass unit equals $1/12^{th}$ of the mass of ${}^{12}C$ atom.
Time taken for the decay of one-half of the initial number of nuclei present in a radioactive sample.
Time at which number of nuclei has been reduced to $e^{-1}$ of its initial value
Measure of the activity of a radioactive source.

subsections.title: Nuclear Density and Probing Methods
subsections.content_text: The density of nuclear matter is independent of the nucleus's size, unlike the mass density of the atom. Furthermore, the nuclear radius determined by electron scattering (which senses charge distribution) differs slightly from that determined by alpha-particle scattering (which senses nuclear matter distribution).

subsections.title: Mass-Energy Conservation and Binding Energy Peaks
subsections.content_text: Einstein's $E = mc^2$ implies a unified law of conservation of mass and energy. Nuclear physics provides the most convincing evidence for this principle. The binding energy per nucleon curve shows that exothermic reactions occur when light nuclei fuse or heavy nuclei undergo fission. The curve is generally smooth but exhibits peaks at specific nuclides like ${}^4He$ and ${}^{16}O$, indicating shell-like structures in nuclei.

subsections.title: Fusion and Antiparticles
subsections.content_text: Nuclear fusion requires extremely high temperatures because light nuclei must have sufficient initial energy to overcome the mutual Coulomb potential barrier. Additionally, particles like the electron and positron form particle-antiparticle pairs; they are identical in mass but have opposite charges. When they meet, they annihilate, converting their mass into energy as gamma-ray photons.

subsections.title: Nuclear Stability and Neutron-Proton Ratio
subsections.content_text: Radioactivity indicates nuclear instability. For light nuclei, stability requires a neutron-to-proton ratio of approximately $1:1$. This ratio increases to about $3:2$ for heavy nuclei to overcome the increased proton-proton repulsion. Only about $10\%$ of known isotopes are stable; others are produced artificially or observed in astronomical phenomena.

sections.title: Reference Data and Problem Solving in Nuclear Physics

subsections.title: Physical Constants and Atomic Masses
subsections.content_text: To solve numerical problems in nuclear physics, several fundamental constants and atomic masses are required. These include the elementary charge $e$, Avogadro's number $N$, the Coulomb constant, and Boltzmann's constant $k$. Conversion factors for energy (MeV to Joules) and mass-energy equivalence (u to MeV/$c^2$) are also essential. The following table summarizes these values as provided in the textbook.

tables.label: Table 13.1
tables.caption: Useful Data for Nuclear Physics Exercises

subsections.title: The Q-value of a Nuclear Reaction
subsections.content_text: The $Q$ value of a nuclear reaction $A + b \rightarrow C + d$ is defined as the difference between the rest mass energy of the reactants and the products: $Q = [m_A + m_b - m_C - m_d]c^2$. Here, the masses refer to the respective nuclei. The $Q$-value determines the energetics of the reaction: if $Q > 0$, the reaction is exothermic (releases energy); if $Q < 0$, the reaction is endothermic (requires energy input).

subsections.title: Nuclear Potential Barrier
subsections.content_text: When two nuclei approach each other, they experience a repulsive electrostatic force. The height of the potential barrier for a head-on collision is given by the Coulomb repulsion energy at the point where the nuclei just touch. Assuming the nuclei are hard spheres of radius $R$, the distance between their centers at contact is $2R$. The potential energy $V$ is calculated as $V = \frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{2R}$.

subsections.title: Nuclear Matter Density
subsections.content_text: Using the empirical relation for the nuclear radius $R = R_0 A^{1/3}$, where $R_0$ is a constant and $A$ is the mass number, we can show that nuclear density is nearly constant. The volume of the nucleus is $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 = \frac{4}{3}\pi R_0^3 A$. The mass of the nucleus is approximately $A \times m_u$. Thus, the density $\rho = \frac{A m_u}{\frac{4}{3}\pi R_0^3 A} = \frac{3 m_u}{4\pi R_0^3}$. Since $m_u$ and $R_0$ are constants, the density $\rho$ is independent of the mass number $A$.

items.problem: Obtain the binding energy (in MeV) of a nitrogen nucleus ($^{14}_7\text{N}$), given $m(^{14}_7\text{N}) = 14.00307$ u.

Obtain the binding energy of the nuclei $^{56}_{26}\text{Fe}$ and $^{209}_{83}\text{Bi}$ in units of MeV from the following data: $m(^{56}_{26}\text{Fe}) = 55.934939$ u, $m(^{209}_{83}\text{Bi}) = 208.980388$ u.

A given coin has a mass of 3.0 g. Calculate the nuclear energy that would be required to separate all the neutrons and protons from each other. For simplicity assume that the coin is entirely made of $^{63}_{29}\text{Cu}$ atoms (of mass 62.92960 u).
Obtain approximately the ratio of the nuclear radii of the gold isotope $^{197}_{79}\text{Au}$ and the silver isotope $^{107}_{47}\text{Ag}$.

The $Q$ value of a nuclear reaction $A + b \rightarrow C + d$ is defined by $Q = [m_A + m_b - m_C - m_d]c^2$ where the masses refer to the respective nuclei. Determine from the given data the $Q$-value of the following reactions and state whether the reactions are exothermic or endothermic. (i) $^1_1\text{H} + ^3_1\text{H} \rightarrow ^2_1\text{H} + ^2_1\text{H}$ (ii) $^{12}_6\text{C} + ^{12}_6\text{C} \rightarrow ^{20}_{10}\text{Ne} + ^4_2\text{He}$. Atomic masses are given to be $m(^2_1\text{H}) = 2.014102$ u, $m(^3_1\text{H}) = 3.016049$ u, $m(^{12}_6\text{C}) = 12.000000$ u, $m(^{20}_{10}\text{Ne}) = 19.992439$ u.

Suppose, we think of fission of a $^{56}_{26}\text{Fe}$ nucleus into two equal fragments, $^{28}_{13}\text{Al}$. Is the fission energetically possible? Argue by working out $Q$ of the process. Given $m(^{56}_{26}\text{Fe}) = 55.93494$ u and $m(^{28}_{13}\text{Al}) = 27.98191$ u.

The fission properties of $^{239}_{94}\text{Pu}$ are very similar to those of $^{235}_{92}\text{U}$. The average energy released per fission is 180 MeV. How much energy, in MeV, is released if all the atoms in 1 kg of pure $^{239}_{94}\text{Pu}$ undergo fission?
How long can an electric lamp of 100W be kept glowing by fusion of 2.0 kg of deuterium? Take the fusion reaction as $^2_1\text{H} + ^2_1\text{H} \rightarrow ^3_2\text{He} + n + 3.27 \text{ MeV}$.

Calculate the height of the potential barrier for a head on collision of two deuterons. (Hint: The height of the potential barrier is given by the Coulomb repulsion between the two deuterons when they just touch each other. Assume that they can be taken as hard spheres of radius 2.0 fm.)
From the relation $R = R_0 A^{1/3}$, where $R_0$ is a constant and $A$ is the mass number of a nucleus, show that the nuclear matter density is nearly constant (i.e. independent of $A$).
